[0:04] What's up geeks and welcome to the channel! In the previous video of this series we discussed
[0:10] and implemented the factory method pattern so as a follow-up we are going to tackle today
[0:14] the Abstract Factory Pattern, therefore if you haven't
[0:17] watched our previous upload or have no idea what the factory method pattern is
[0:22] I suggest you take a look at the video link down below in the description first.
[0:26] So the abstract factory is a creational design pattern
[0:30] that lets you produce families of related objects without specifying their concrete classes
[0:36] you can think of it as if you were using the factory method pattern but multiple times
[0:40] or as if you were scaling up your factory to handle the creation of multiple distinct products.
[0:47] Okay as usual and to better understand the concept at hand
[0:50] let's take a practical approach and imagine you want to simulate the creation of computer parts.
[0:56] Now to not overcomplicate the example right from the beginning
[0:59] suppose we are just manufacturing GPUs Now, the GPUs you are manufacturing

[2:44] and then let each different concrete product implement this interface.

[3:25] Now some of you may be wondering how did this guy manage to violate the open-closed principle even
[3:30] though he was applying the factory method pattern? Well you see in this particular
[3:34] example a single factory is not enough and that is where you should start thinking
[3:39] about scaling up your implementation from the factory method pattern
[3:43] to the abstract factory design pattern. To do this let's bring back our GPU interface
[3:48] and create a separate interface called Monitor this interface will be implemented by the
[3:53] AsusMonitor and MsiMonitor classes you see The first thing the abstract factory
[3:58] pattern suggests is to explicitly declare interfaces for each distinct product we have
[4:04] and then make all variants of these products follow those interfaces.
[4:08] The next suggested move is to create inside our abstract factory which
[4:12] in our case will be the Company class a list of creation methods or factory
[4:17] methods one for each abstract product we have in this example we have two abstract products
[4:22] the GPU and the Monitor interfaces hence we will have two factory methods

[4:34] and that is how we decouple the creation of our products from the part that is actually using it

[5:10] classes in this example each concrete factory

[5:23] correspond or relate to the type of factory it was created from
[5:27] So to recap you should use the abstract factory pattern when your code needs to
[5:31] work with various families of related products but you don't want it to depend on the concrete
[5:37] classes of those products though they might be known beforehand
[5:40] and that's why many designs start by using the factory method pattern as it is less
[5:45] complicated and more customizable via subclasses and later evolve toward an abstract factory design
[5:52] By using this pattern you will be following and applying the open-closed principle
[5:56] and the single responsibility principle as you are centralizing the product
[6:00] creation code in one place in the program. So that's it for this video, I hope it was
